seoTitle: How to Block a Country in Cloudflare (2026)
metaDescription: Restrict traffic from specific countries using Cloudflare's WAF Custom Rules, choosing between a hard block, challenge, or rate limit.
intro: If you're seeing abuse or spam traffic concentrated from certain regions, Cloudflare's WAF lets you filter it out by country. This guide covers building a geo-location firewall rule and choosing the right mitigation action for your situation.

steps.heading: Open the WAF section
steps.body: Select your domain, go to the Security directory, and click into WAF.

steps.heading: Create a new rule
steps.body: Select Custom Rules and click 'Create rule' to open the expression builder.

steps.heading: Set the field to Country
steps.body: Name your rule and use the Field dropdown to select the 'Country' selector.

steps.heading: Choose your target countries
steps.body: Pick an operator like 'equals' or 'in' and check the boxes for the specific countries you want to restrict.

steps.heading: Choose your action and deploy
steps.body: Set the action to Block, Managed Challenge, or a rate-limiting rule depending on how strict you want the restriction, then deploy and monitor the security events log.

faq.question: Should I always use a hard Block instead of a Challenge?
faq.answer: A hard Block denies access entirely, while a Managed Challenge lets legitimate visitors through after passing a verification step; the right choice depends on whether you want to fully exclude a region or just filter out bots.

faq.question: Can I block multiple countries in one rule?
faq.answer: Yes, using the 'in' operator lets you check boxes for several countries within a single custom rule.

faq.question: Will blocking a country affect search engine crawlers based there?
faq.answer: It can, since geo-blocking rules apply to all traffic from that country unless you add exceptions, so consider allowing known crawler IP ranges if that's a concern.

faq.question: How do I check if the rule is actually blocking traffic?
faq.answer: Review your Security Events log after deploying the rule to see live traffic being matched and blocked by your country filter.
